questions.question: Which of the following BEST defines marketing?

options.A: The process of selling goods to customers
options.B: All activities involved in identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs profitably
options.C: The advertising of products through various media
options.D: The distribution of goods from producer to consumer

questions.explanation: Marketing encompasses all activities from identifying customer needs, developing products, pricing, promoting, and distributing them to satisfy those needs profitably.

questions.question: The marketing concept holds that

options.A: Producing in large quantities reduces cost and increases profit
options.B: A firm should focus on the needs and wants of customers and satisfy them better than competitors
options.C: Aggressive selling is the key to business success
options.D: Firms should produce high-quality products regardless of customer needs

questions.explanation: The marketing concept is a customer-centred philosophy that prioritises identifying and satisfying customer needs profitably.

questions.question: The selling concept differs from the marketing concept in that the selling concept

options.A: Focuses on customer satisfaction above everything else
options.B: Starts with customer needs and works backward to the product
options.C: Focuses on aggressively selling whatever is produced rather than what customers want
options.D: Emphasises long-term customer relationships

questions.explanation: The selling concept assumes consumers must be persuaded to buy what firms produce. The marketing concept starts with customer needs.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the traditional 4Ps of the marketing mix?

options.A: Product
options.B: People
options.C: Price
options.D: Promotion

questions.explanation: The traditional 4Ps are Product, Price, Place, and Promotion. 'People' is part of the extended 7Ps used for services marketing.

questions.question: The extended marketing mix for services (7Ps) adds which three additional elements to the traditional 4Ps?

options.A: Planning, Positioning, Packaging
options.B: People, Process, Physical Evidence
options.C: Publicity, Partnerships, Packaging
options.D: Profit, Public Relations, Penetration

questions.explanation: The 7Ps extend the 4Ps with People (staff and customers), Process (service delivery systems), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues of service quality).

questions.question: A market is BEST described in marketing as

options.A: A physical location where goods are bought and sold
options.B: All actual and potential buyers who share a need that can be satisfied through exchange
options.C: A government-regulated trading zone
options.D: Only consumers who have already purchased a product

questions.explanation: In marketing, a market comprises all actual and potential buyers who share a need that can be met through exchange.

questions.question: Societal marketing extends the marketing concept by also considering

options.A: The profits of shareholders
options.B: The long-term well-being of consumers and society, not just immediate wants
options.C: Only the needs of business customers
options.D: Government regulations on advertising

questions.explanation: The societal marketing concept balances three considerations: company profits, consumer wants, and society's long-run interests.

questions.question: A need in marketing is

options.A: A desire for something not currently possessed
options.B: A basic state of felt deprivation — physical, social, or individual
options.C: A demand backed by purchasing power
options.D: A specific product wanted by a consumer

questions.explanation: A need is a basic state of felt deprivation. A want is a need shaped by culture. A demand is a want backed by purchasing power.

questions.question: Exchange in marketing refers to

options.A: Returning goods to a seller for a refund
options.B: The act of obtaining a desired object from someone by offering something in return
options.C: Currency exchange between buyers and sellers in different countries
options.D: The transfer of ownership of goods between businesses

questions.explanation: Exchange is the core concept of marketing — obtaining a desired product by offering something of value in return.

questions.question: The production concept assumes that consumers prefer products that are

options.A: Heavily advertised and promoted
options.B: Widely available and affordable, so the firm focuses on high production efficiency
options.C: Unique and customised to individual needs
options.D: Ecologically sustainable

questions.explanation: The production concept holds that consumers favour products that are available and affordable, so management focuses on improving production and distribution efficiency.

questions.question: The product concept assumes that consumers favour products that

options.A: Are the cheapest available
options.B: Offer the most quality, performance, and features, leading firms to focus on continuous product improvement
options.C: Are most widely distributed
options.D: Are aggressively advertised

questions.explanation: The product concept assumes buyers want the highest quality, so firms focus on making superior products (marketing myopia risk: ignoring the broader customer need).

questions.question: A market-oriented firm consistently focuses on

options.A: Producing goods as efficiently as possible
options.B: The needs of customers and coordinates all activities to satisfy those needs profitably
options.C: Setting prices based on what the market will bear
options.D: Placing products in as many locations as possible

questions.explanation: A market-oriented firm puts the customer at the centre of all business decisions, conducting research, designing products, and setting prices based on customer needs.

questions.question: Demarketing refers to marketing aimed at

options.A: Eliminating marketing expenses to save costs
options.B: Reducing demand for a product — used when demand exceeds supply or to discourage unhealthy behaviour
options.C: Marketing products that have been withdrawn from the market
options.D: Marketing products below cost to reduce stock levels

questions.explanation: Demarketing deliberately tries to reduce demand — e.g., governments discouraging smoking or utilities discouraging peak-time energy use.

questions.question: The ultimate goal of marketing is to

options.A: Maximise advertising expenditure
options.B: Create and deliver superior value to customers while achieving the organisation's objectives
options.C: Eliminate competition from the market
options.D: Produce goods at the lowest possible cost

questions.explanation: The ultimate goal of marketing is to create superior customer value — satisfying customers better than competitors — while achieving the organisation's financial and strategic objectives.

questions.question: Marketing research is BEST defined as

options.A: The systematic gathering, recording, and analysis of data about problems relating to the marketing of goods and services
options.B: The process of interviewing customers about their preferences
options.C: The study of competitor pricing and promotional strategies
options.D: The development of new products based on existing technology

questions.explanation: Marketing research is the systematic design, collection, analysis, and reporting of data relevant to a specific marketing situation.

questions.question: Primary data in marketing research refers to

options.A: Data collected from published government statistics
options.B: Original data collected for the specific research problem at hand
options.C: Data obtained from competitors' reports
options.D: Information from industry trade associations

questions.explanation: Primary data is original, first-hand information collected specifically for the current research problem through surveys, interviews, focus groups, or observation.

questions.question: Secondary data in marketing research is

options.A: Data collected through personal interviews with customers
options.B: Data that already exists and was collected for a different purpose but is relevant to the current research
options.C: Data collected through experimental methods
options.D: The second set of data collected to confirm primary findings

questions.explanation: Secondary data is existing information — internal (company records) or external (government statistics, trade publications). It is cheaper and faster to obtain than primary data.

questions.question: A focus group in marketing research is

options.A: A large-scale quantitative survey of thousands of respondents
options.B: A small group of selected individuals brought together to discuss a product or topic in depth
options.C: A panel of marketing experts who evaluate new products
options.D: A group of sales representatives reporting on customer reactions

questions.explanation: A focus group is a qualitative method where 6-12 selected participants discuss a topic in depth, guided by a moderator, providing rich insights into attitudes and motivations.

questions.question: Test marketing involves

options.A: Testing a product concept with a small focus group
options.B: Introducing a product to a limited market to evaluate its performance before a full national launch
options.C: Testing the durability of a product in a laboratory
options.D: Comparing a product's features with competitor products

questions.explanation: Test marketing launches a new product in selected markets to assess consumer response and identify problems before a full national rollout.

questions.question: Random sampling means

options.A: The researcher selects whoever is convenient to interview
options.B: Every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected
options.C: The researcher selects respondents based on specific characteristics
options.D: A sample is chosen from each segment proportionally

questions.explanation: In random (probability) sampling, every member of the target population has an equal and known chance of being included, reducing bias.

questions.question: Quota sampling is a type of non-probability sampling where

options.A: Respondents are selected randomly from a list
options.B: Interviewers are given quotas of respondents to interview from specified groups
options.C: Every nth person on a list is selected
options.D: All members of a specific group are interviewed

questions.explanation: In quota sampling, interviewers fill predetermined quotas from defined subgroups. It is non-random and cheaper than probability sampling.

questions.question: Quantitative research in marketing collects

options.A: In-depth opinions and feelings through small samples
options.B: Numerical data from large samples that can be statistically analysed
options.C: Information from company financial records
options.D: Data through informal conversations with a few customers

questions.explanation: Quantitative research gathers numerical data through surveys with large samples, enabling statistical analysis and generalisation.

questions.question: Qualitative research in marketing collects

options.A: Large volumes of numerical data for statistical analysis
options.B: In-depth, non-numerical insights into attitudes and motivations through small samples
options.C: Data from government census records
options.D: Sales figures and market share statistics

questions.explanation: Qualitative research gathers non-numerical insights — understanding why people think and behave as they do — through focus groups, depth interviews, and observation.

questions.question: Observation as a method of marketing research involves

options.A: Asking customers to complete a written questionnaire
options.B: Recording the behaviour of consumers without directly questioning them
options.C: Discussing products with a small group of consumers
options.D: Testing a new product concept with potential buyers

questions.explanation: Observation research watches and records consumer behaviour without asking questions — e.g., tracking eye movements on websites or watching shoppers in a store.

questions.question: A consumer panel in marketing research is

options.A: A group of consumers recruited to discuss a product in a single session
options.B: A fixed group who provide data on their purchases or attitudes at regular intervals over time
options.C: A group of marketing experts who evaluate advertising campaigns
options.D: A committee of government officials who regulate consumer complaints

questions.explanation: A consumer panel is a standing group that regularly provides data on purchases or attitudes, enabling tracking of trends over time.

questions.question: Market segmentation is defined as

options.A: Dividing a market into distinct groups of buyers who have different needs and may require separate products or marketing mixes
options.B: Setting different prices for different customers
options.C: The process of identifying the best market for a product
options.D: Reducing the size of a market through advertising

questions.explanation: Market segmentation divides a heterogeneous market into smaller, homogeneous segments of buyers with similar needs, allowing targeted marketing.

questions.question: Which of the following is a basis for demographic segmentation?

options.A: Lifestyle and personality
options.B: Geographical region
options.C: Age, gender, income, and family size
options.D: Purchase behaviour and brand loyalty

questions.explanation: Demographic segmentation divides the market based on measurable population characteristics — age, gender, income, education, occupation, and family size.

questions.question: Psychographic segmentation divides the market based on

options.A: Age and gender
options.B: Geographic location
options.C: Lifestyle, values, personality, and social class
options.D: Purchase frequency and brand loyalty

questions.explanation: Psychographic segmentation uses psychological characteristics — lifestyle, values, attitudes, and personality — to create meaningful consumer segments.

questions.question: Behavioural segmentation divides buyers based on

options.A: Their age, income, and education level
options.B: Their geographic location and climate
options.C: Their knowledge, attitudes, usage, or responses to a product
options.D: Their cultural values and lifestyle

questions.explanation: Behavioural segmentation groups buyers by their behaviour toward products — occasions, benefits sought, usage rate, loyalty status, and buyer readiness stage.

questions.question: A niche market is

options.A: A very large mass market with millions of consumers
options.B: A small, well-defined segment with specific needs not adequately served by mainstream products
options.C: A market for luxury goods sold at premium prices
options.D: A new market not yet discovered by competitors

questions.explanation: A niche market is a narrowly defined segment with specific needs. Niche marketers focus on serving a small segment extremely well, often achieving high margins.

questions.question: Targeting in marketing refers to

options.A: Setting sales targets for the sales force
options.B: Evaluating market segments and selecting one or more to enter
options.C: Promoting products to all consumers
options.D: Setting a price that targets a specific profit margin

questions.explanation: Targeting involves evaluating each segment's attractiveness and selecting which segment(s) the firm will serve.

questions.question: Undifferentiated (mass) marketing strategy involves

options.A: Creating different products for different market segments
options.B: Targeting several segments with different marketing mixes
options.C: Targeting the whole market with one product and one marketing mix
options.D: Focusing on a single narrow market segment

questions.explanation: Mass marketing ignores segment differences and targets the whole market with one offering — e.g., petrol, salt, basic commodities.

questions.question: Differentiated marketing strategy involves

options.A: Targeting the whole market with a single product
options.B: Targeting several market segments with different marketing mixes tailored to each
options.C: Focusing on one narrow market segment only
options.D: Selling the same product at different prices to different customers

questions.explanation: Differentiated marketing targets multiple segments with separate offers for each, increasing total sales but also costs.

questions.question: Concentrated marketing (niche marketing) means

options.A: Using heavy advertising to reach the entire market
options.B: Focusing all marketing effort on one small but well-defined market segment
options.C: Concentrating on price competition in the market
options.D: Using only one distribution channel to reach consumers

questions.explanation: Concentrated marketing commits all resources to serving one segment extremely well, often achieving higher margins but concentrating risk.

questions.question: Geographic segmentation divides the market by

options.A: Income level and social class
options.B: Region, city size, climate, and urban/rural location
options.C: Age, gender, and family size
options.D: Lifestyle and personality

questions.explanation: Geographic segmentation divides the market based on location — country, region, city size, density, or climate.

questions.question: The requirements for effective market segmentation include all EXCEPT

options.A: The segment must be measurable
options.B: The segment must be accessible
options.C: The segment must be profitable enough to serve
options.D: The segment must contain only one type of consumer product

questions.explanation: For effective segmentation, segments must be measurable, accessible, substantial, differentiable, and actionable. The type of products within the segment is not a requirement.

questions.question: Micromarketing (one-to-one marketing) involves

options.A: Targeting all consumers with one standard message
options.B: Tailoring products and programmes to the individual level
options.C: Focusing on the smallest viable market segment
options.D: Marketing products designed for microorganisms

questions.explanation: Micromarketing treats each customer as a unique segment, using CRM technology and data to personalise offerings and communications.

questions.question: Market positioning refers to

options.A: The physical location of a product on a retail shelf
options.B: The place a product occupies in consumers' minds relative to competing products
options.C: The geographic distribution of a product
options.D: The pricing strategy used to position a product

questions.explanation: Positioning is about creating a distinct, desired image in consumers' minds relative to competing products.

questions.question: A perceptual map in marketing is used to

options.A: Map out the geographic distribution of customers
options.B: Visually show how consumers perceive competing brands on two or more key attributes
options.C: Plan the layout of a retail store
options.D: Show the stages in the product life cycle

questions.explanation: A perceptual (positioning) map shows how consumers perceive competing products on key attributes — helping identify positioning gaps and competitive positions.

questions.question: The core product refers to

options.A: The physical good itself — its features and design
options.B: The fundamental benefit or problem-solving service the buyer is really purchasing
options.C: The brand name and reputation associated with the product
options.D: The after-sales service and warranty provided

questions.explanation: The core product is the central benefit sought — e.g., when buying a drill, the core product is the hole (the solution), not the drill itself.

questions.question: The augmented product includes

options.A: Only the physical features and design of the product
options.B: Additional services and benefits beyond the core product — such as warranty, after-sales service, and delivery
options.C: The packaging and labelling only
options.D: The brand name and logo only

questions.explanation: The augmented product adds value through installation, after-sales service, warranty, and delivery, creating differentiation and competitive advantage.

questions.question: A product line is

options.A: A single product offered in one version
options.B: A group of closely related products that function similarly, sold to the same customers, and marketed through the same channels
options.C: All the products offered by a company
options.D: A product being phased out of the market

questions.explanation: A product line is a group of related products — e.g., a range of shampoos by the same brand. Product mix refers to all product lines a company offers.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a consumer convenience good?

options.A: A house
options.B: A luxury car
options.C: A loaf of bread
options.D: An industrial machine

questions.explanation: Convenience goods (bread, soap, newspapers) are frequently purchased, low-cost items bought with minimum effort and comparison.

questions.question: Shopping goods are products that

options.A: Are bought frequently with little thought
options.B: Consumers compare carefully on suitability, quality, price, and style before buying
options.C: Have unique characteristics for which buyers make a special purchase effort
options.D: Consumers do not know about or do not think to buy

questions.explanation: Shopping goods (clothing, furniture, electronics) require more time and comparison before purchase.

questions.question: Specialty goods are

options.A: Products bought frequently at low cost
options.B: Products with unique characteristics for which buyers make a special effort and will not accept substitutes
options.C: Industrial goods used in manufacturing
options.D: Goods consumers are unaware of

questions.explanation: Specialty goods (luxury cars, designer items) have unique features for which buyers will make special purchase effort with little comparison shopping.

questions.question: Branding gives a product

options.A: A higher manufacturing cost
options.B: A name, term, sign, symbol, or design that identifies it and differentiates it from competitors
options.C: A government trademark that prevents copying
options.D: Automatic price leadership in the market

questions.explanation: A brand is a name, term, sign, symbol, or design that identifies the products of one seller and differentiates them from competitors.

questions.question: Brand equity refers to

options.A: The cost of registering a brand name
options.B: The added value a well-known brand name gives to a product beyond its functional value
options.C: The number of products sold under a brand name
options.D: The advertising budget allocated to a brand

questions.explanation: Brand equity is the positive differential effect that knowing the brand name has on customer response — allowing premium pricing and building loyalty.

questions.question: Packaging serves which of the following functions?

options.A: Only to protect the product during transit
options.B: Protection, convenience, communication, and promotion of the product
options.C: To increase the cost of the product
options.D: Only to attract children to the product

questions.explanation: Packaging protects the product, provides convenience, communicates information, and promotes the brand through design and colour at the point of sale.

questions.question: Product mix width refers to

options.A: The number of variants within each product line
options.B: The total number of product lines offered by the company
options.C: The total number of individual products in all lines
options.D: The quality range within each product line

questions.explanation: Product mix width is the number of different product lines a company offers. Depth refers to variants per line; length to total items.

questions.question: Product differentiation means

options.A: Reducing product quality to lower costs
options.B: Making a product meaningfully different from competitors' products in the minds of buyers
options.C: Offering the same product at different prices in different markets
options.D: Developing several versions of a product for different income groups

questions.explanation: Product differentiation creates perceived differences through features, quality, design, brand, or service — justifying a price premium and building loyalty.

questions.question: The product life cycle (PLC) describes

options.A: The physical lifespan of a product until it breaks down
options.B: The course of a product's sales and profits over its lifetime — from introduction to decline
options.C: The time taken to manufacture a product from raw materials
options.D: The warranty period offered with a product

questions.explanation: The PLC shows how a product's sales and profits evolve through Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline stages, each requiring different marketing strategies.

questions.question: During the introduction stage of the product life cycle

options.A: Sales are at their peak and profits are high
options.B: Sales grow slowly, profits are low or negative, and heavy promotion is needed to create awareness
options.C: Competition is intense and firms focus on price cuts
options.D: The firm begins to discontinue the product

questions.explanation: In the introduction stage, sales grow slowly, heavy promotion investment is needed, costs are high, and the product usually runs at a loss initially.

questions.question: In the growth stage of the product life cycle

options.A: Sales fall rapidly and the product is withdrawn
options.B: Sales rise rapidly, profits increase, and new competitors enter the market
options.C: Sales are stable and focus is on maintaining market share
options.D: The product is introduced to the market for the first time

questions.explanation: During growth, sales and profits rise rapidly as more customers adopt the product and new competitors are attracted.

questions.question: The maturity stage of the product life cycle is characterised by

options.A: Rapid sales growth and high profits
options.B: Declining sales and heavy losses
options.C: Sales growth slowing, profits stabilising, and intense competition
options.D: The product being newly introduced to the market

questions.explanation: In maturity, sales level off, competition intensifies, prices and profits decline, and firms focus on market defence and differentiation.

questions.question: During the decline stage of the product life cycle, a firm may choose to

options.A: Invest heavily in advertising to revive sales
options.B: Harvest or divest — reducing costs and milking remaining sales, or withdrawing the product
options.C: Lower prices aggressively to maintain market share
options.D: Expand distribution to new markets

questions.explanation: In decline, firms typically harvest (reduce costs, maintain price) or divest (discontinue). Some may attempt revitalisation through innovation.

questions.question: Extension strategies for a product in the decline stage include

options.A: Withdrawing the product from the market immediately
options.B: Finding new uses, new markets, new customers, or modifying the product to extend its life
options.C: Increasing the price to maximise profit before withdrawal
options.D: Reducing quality to cut costs

questions.explanation: Extension strategies delay decline by finding new markets, new uses, or by modifying/repositioning the product.

questions.question: The first stage in the new product development (NPD) process is

options.A: Concept testing
options.B: Idea generation
options.C: Business analysis
options.D: Test marketing

questions.explanation: NPD begins with idea generation, followed by screening, concept testing, business analysis, product development, test marketing, and commercialisation.

questions.question: Concept testing in NPD involves

options.A: Testing the physical prototype of the product
options.B: Testing the product concept with a group of target consumers to gauge their reaction
options.C: Testing the product in a limited geographic market
options.D: Testing the manufacturing process for quality

questions.explanation: Concept testing presents a product concept to target consumers to assess interest and purchase intent before expensive product development begins.

questions.question: The innovation adoption curve classifies consumers. 'Early adopters' are

options.A: The very first consumers to try a new product
options.B: Opinion leaders who adopt early and influence others — the second group after innovators
options.C: The last group to adopt a new product
options.D: Consumers who try a product only after the majority has adopted it

questions.explanation: Early adopters (13.5%) are opinion leaders who adopt early and influence the early majority. The sequence is: Innovators → Early Adopters → Early Majority → Late Majority → Laggards.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common reason for new product failure?

options.A: Too much investment in market research
options.B: Overestimated market size, poor marketing, or poor product quality
options.C: Setting the price too low
options.D: Entering the market too early before competitors

questions.explanation: Common causes of new product failure include overestimated market size, poor positioning, design problems, aggressive competitive response, or insufficient marketing support.

questions.question: Which of the following is a cost-based pricing method?

options.A: Competitive pricing
options.B: Perceived value pricing
options.C: Cost-plus (mark-up) pricing
options.D: Psychological pricing

questions.explanation: Cost-plus pricing adds a standard mark-up to the cost of producing or buying the product, ensuring all costs are covered.

questions.question: A price skimming strategy involves

options.A: Setting a very low initial price to attract many buyers quickly
options.B: Setting a high initial price when launching a new product, targeting early adopters willing to pay a premium
options.C: Setting prices at the same level as major competitors
options.D: Setting price based on consumer psychology

questions.explanation: Price skimming sets a high launch price and skims maximum revenue from each segment willing to pay. Common for innovative or luxury products.

questions.question: A penetration pricing strategy involves

options.A: Setting a very high initial price to signal premium quality
options.B: Setting a low initial price to penetrate the market quickly and gain market share
options.C: Setting prices above competitors to signal superior quality
options.D: Setting prices equal to competitors

questions.explanation: Penetration pricing sets a low launch price to rapidly gain market share, effective in price-sensitive markets with economies of scale.

questions.question: Psychological pricing uses prices like ₦999 instead of ₦1,000 because

options.A: It reduces tax paid by the seller
options.B: Consumers perceive ₦999 as significantly cheaper even though the difference is minimal
options.C: It is cheaper to print prices ending in 9
options.D: Government regulations require prices ending in odd numbers

questions.explanation: Psychological (odd-even) pricing exploits the perception that ₦999 is much less than ₦1,000. Consumers process the left digit first, making the price seem meaningfully cheaper.

questions.question: Price discrimination refers to

options.A: Charging higher prices for superior quality goods
options.B: Charging different prices to different customer segments for the same product
options.C: Setting prices based on the cost of serving different segments
options.D: Reducing prices during sale periods

questions.explanation: Price discrimination involves charging different prices to different buyers for the same product — e.g., different cinema prices for adults and students — to maximise revenue.

questions.question: Break-even pricing sets a price at which

options.A: The firm makes maximum profit
options.B: Total revenue equals total costs — no profit or loss is made
options.C: The price is equal to the competitor's price
options.D: The firm recovers only variable costs

questions.explanation: Break-even pricing finds the price at which total revenue equals total costs and is used as a starting point for pricing decisions.

questions.question: Value-based pricing sets price based on

options.A: The cost of producing the product
options.B: The buyer's perceived value of the product rather than production costs
options.C: The prices set by the leading competitor
options.D: The price that achieves break-even fastest

questions.explanation: Value-based pricing sets prices based on what customers believe the product is worth, requiring deep understanding of customer value perceptions.

questions.question: Captive product pricing (razor-and-blades pricing) involves

options.A: Pricing a product below cost to attract customers
options.B: Setting a low price for a core product but charging a high price for required accessories or supplies
options.C: Setting different prices in different countries
options.D: Bundling several products together at a combined price

questions.explanation: Captive product pricing charges a low price for the main product but high prices for the captive consumable accessories — e.g., cheap printers but expensive ink cartridges.

questions.question: Bundle pricing involves

options.A: Charging different prices to different customer groups
options.B: Combining several products and selling them at a single reduced price
options.C: Offering a product at a temporarily reduced price
options.D: Setting a very low initial price and raising it later

questions.explanation: Bundle pricing combines products into one package at a lower combined price — e.g., a meal deal. It encourages purchase of multiple items and increases total revenue.

questions.question: Loss-leader pricing involves

options.A: Setting prices below cost on selected items to attract customers who then buy other profitable items
options.B: Setting the lowest possible price to eliminate all competitors
options.C: Setting prices at a loss to remain competitive
options.D: Pricing new products at a loss until break-even

questions.explanation: Loss-leader pricing sets prices below cost on key items to attract customers to the store, where they buy other full-priced items that generate profit.

questions.question: Promotional pricing includes which of the following?

options.A: Setting a permanent low price for the product
options.B: Temporarily reducing prices through special offers, discounts, and sales events to boost short-term sales
options.C: Setting prices based on government subsidy rates
options.D: Using cost-plus pricing for all promotional products

questions.explanation: Promotional pricing involves temporary price reductions — seasonal sales, BOGOF, loss-leader pricing — to stimulate short-term purchases.

questions.question: Price elasticity of demand measures

options.A: The speed at which prices change in response to cost increases
options.B: The degree to which demand for a product changes in response to a change in price
options.C: The flexibility of a firm's pricing policy
options.D: The difference between wholesale and retail prices

questions.explanation: PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price. If PED > 1, demand is elastic; if PED < 1, demand is inelastic. Pricing strategy depends heavily on PED.

questions.question: If a product has inelastic demand, a price increase will

options.A: Cause a large fall in quantity demanded
options.B: Cause a small fall in quantity demanded and increase total revenue
options.C: Not change total revenue at all
options.D: Cause buyers to switch to alternatives immediately

questions.explanation: With inelastic demand (PED < 1), a price increase leads to a proportionally smaller decrease in quantity, so total revenue increases.

questions.question: Contribution pricing means

options.A: Setting a price that covers all costs and contributes to charity
options.B: Setting a price above variable cost so the product makes a positive contribution to covering fixed costs and profit
options.C: Setting prices based on contributions from shareholders
options.D: Pricing below cost to generate customer loyalty

questions.explanation: Contribution pricing ensures the selling price exceeds variable cost, so each unit sold contributes to covering fixed costs. Useful for marginal decisions.

questions.question: Competitive pricing means

options.A: Setting prices below all competitors
options.B: Setting prices based on what competitors charge in the market
options.C: Setting prices based on production cost
options.D: Setting different prices for different customers

questions.explanation: Competitive (going-rate) pricing sets prices based on competitors' prices. Common in oligopolies where price leadership exists.

questions.question: Skimming pricing is most appropriate for products that are

options.A: Widely available commodities with many substitutes
options.B: New, innovative products with few substitutes and early adopters willing to pay a premium
options.C: Basic necessities that all consumers need
options.D: Products in the decline stage with falling demand

questions.explanation: Price skimming works when the product is innovative with few substitutes, a segment of buyers is willing to pay a premium, and the firm wants to recoup R&D investment quickly.

questions.question: A distribution channel (marketing channel) is

options.A: A television or radio channel used for advertising
options.B: A set of interdependent organisations involved in making a product available to the consumer
options.C: The pipeline through which goods are physically transported
options.D: A government-approved route for distributing essential goods

questions.explanation: A distribution (marketing) channel is the network of intermediaries that move a product from the producer to the final consumer.

questions.question: Intensive distribution means

options.A: Selling through only one exclusive retailer in each area
options.B: Stocking the product in as many outlets as possible to maximise availability
options.C: Selling through a carefully selected number of outlets
options.D: Distributing goods directly from manufacturer to consumer

questions.explanation: Intensive distribution places the product in as many retail outlets as possible — e.g., soft drinks, cigarettes — to ensure maximum availability.

questions.question: Exclusive distribution means

options.A: Only high-income customers can buy the product
options.B: The manufacturer grants only one retailer or dealer in a given area the right to sell the product
options.C: The product is only available online
options.D: The product is restricted to government-approved retailers

questions.explanation: Exclusive distribution grants a single dealer exclusive rights in a territory, building a prestigious image and giving better control over marketing — e.g., luxury cars, designer goods.

questions.question: Selective distribution involves

options.A: Distributing through all available retail outlets
options.B: Using a carefully chosen limited number of retailers who meet specific criteria
options.C: Selling exclusively through one distributor per region
options.D: Distributing only to qualifying customers based on income

questions.explanation: Selective distribution uses more than one but fewer than all willing outlets, balancing coverage and prestige — e.g., branded electrical appliances in selected stores.

questions.question: A direct marketing channel (zero-level channel) is one where

options.A: All goods are sold through agents
options.B: The producer sells directly to the consumer with no intermediaries
options.C: Goods pass through wholesaler and retailer to reach the consumer
options.D: The manufacturer uses an agent to reach retailers

questions.explanation: A zero-level (direct) channel means the manufacturer sells directly to end consumers through its own stores, websites, or sales force.

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of using intermediaries in the distribution channel?

options.A: The manufacturer has more control over final prices
options.B: Intermediaries reduce the number of transactions and provide wider market reach at lower cost
options.C: The manufacturer builds closer relationships with end consumers
options.D: Intermediaries guarantee a fixed level of sales

questions.explanation: Intermediaries reduce the total number of transactions, provide specialised services, and give manufacturers wider geographic reach than they could achieve alone.

questions.question: Physical distribution management is concerned with

options.A: The design and placement of advertising billboards
options.B: The efficient movement and storage of goods from producer to consumer — including transport, warehousing, and inventory management
options.C: The management of the sales force
options.D: The hiring and training of distribution staff

questions.explanation: Physical distribution (logistics) management involves planning the efficient movement of goods from origin to consumer — transport, warehousing, order processing, and inventory control.

questions.question: Supply chain management involves

options.A: Managing the company's financial resources efficiently
options.B: Coordinating the flow of materials, information, and finances from suppliers through production to the final customer
options.C: Selecting the most suitable advertising media
options.D: Managing relationships with individual consumers

questions.explanation: SCM optimises the entire chain from raw material sourcing to end-consumer delivery — improving efficiency, reducing costs, and enhancing customer satisfaction.

questions.question: E-commerce as a distribution channel has which of the following advantages?

options.A: It limits the geographic reach of the seller
options.B: It eliminates the need for any marketing effort
options.C: It reduces distribution costs, extends geographic reach, and allows 24/7 trading
options.D: It guarantees higher profit margins than physical stores

questions.explanation: E-commerce reduces overhead costs, allows global reach, operates around the clock, and can personalise the shopping experience.

questions.question: A Vertical Marketing System (VMS) is

options.A: A distribution system where all channel members act independently
options.B: A distribution system where producers, wholesalers, and retailers act as a unified system with one channel leader
options.C: A distribution system that only sells through vending machines
options.D: A system that sells products both vertically and horizontally

questions.explanation: A VMS is a professionally managed, unified distribution system where one channel member leads and coordinates the entire channel for efficiency and marketing impact.

questions.question: Channel conflict occurs when

options.A: A company uses too many distribution channels simultaneously
options.B: Channel members disagree over goals, roles, or rewards — one member feels another is hurting their interests
options.C: A product fails to reach consumers on time due to transport disruption
options.D: Two competitors use the same distribution channels

questions.explanation: Channel conflict arises when one member's actions adversely affect another — e.g., a manufacturer selling directly online at lower prices than its retailers.

questions.question: The promotional mix (communications mix) consists of

options.A: Product, price, place, and promotion
options.B: Advertising, personal selling, sales promotion, public relations, and direct marketing
options.C: Television, radio, newspapers, and social media
options.D: Discounts, coupons, samples, and competitions

questions.explanation: The promotional mix includes: advertising, personal selling, sales promotion, public relations, and direct marketing — each with different strengths.

questions.question: Advertising is defined as

options.A: A face-to-face interaction between a salesperson and a prospect
options.B: Any paid form of non-personal presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor
options.C: A temporary price reduction to boost sales
options.D: Publicity generated by press releases and media coverage

questions.explanation: Advertising is paid, non-personal communication through mass media by an identified sponsor. It builds awareness and reaches large audiences simultaneously.

questions.question: Personal selling is

options.A: Placing advertisements in national newspapers
options.B: The personal interaction between a salesperson and a customer to make a sale and build a relationship
options.C: Selling products through vending machines
options.D: Using a website to sell products to individual customers

questions.explanation: Personal selling involves direct, person-to-person interaction where the salesperson presents the product, overcomes objections, and closes the sale.

questions.question: Sales promotion includes

options.A: Long-term brand-building advertising
options.B: Short-term incentives to encourage immediate purchase — such as coupons, discounts, competitions, and free samples
options.C: Press releases and editorial coverage
options.D: Direct mail campaigns sent to existing customers

questions.explanation: Sales promotion comprises short-term incentives that stimulate quick purchases — BOGOF, coupons, competitions, loyalty points, and in-store demonstrations.

questions.question: Public relations (PR) in marketing involves

options.A: Paying for advertising space in newspapers
options.B: Building good relations with publics by obtaining favourable publicity and managing unfavourable events
options.C: Recruiting and training the salesforce
options.D: Setting prices that maintain the company's public image

questions.explanation: PR builds and maintains a favourable image through press releases, events, sponsorships, and community relations. Unlike advertising, good PR coverage is unpaid (earned media).

questions.question: Direct marketing involves

options.A: Selling goods through retail stores only
options.B: Communicating directly with targeted individual consumers to obtain an immediate response
options.C: Using only mass media such as television and radio
options.D: Distributing goods directly from manufacturer to consumer

questions.explanation: Direct marketing bypasses intermediaries to communicate directly with consumers through direct mail, email, telemarketing, and online targeted ads.

questions.question: A push strategy in promotion involves

options.A: Advertising heavily to consumers to pull products through the distribution channel
options.B: Promoting the product to intermediaries who then push it to consumers
options.C: Setting a low price to push consumers to buy
options.D: Using social media to push notifications to consumers

questions.explanation: A push strategy directs promotional effort at the distribution channel — trade promotions and personal selling to retailers/wholesalers — encouraging them to stock and recommend the product.

questions.question: A pull strategy in promotion involves

options.A: Giving discounts to wholesalers and retailers
options.B: Advertising heavily to end consumers to create demand that pulls the product through the distribution channel
options.C: Using a sales force to push products to retailers
options.D: Reducing price to attract bulk purchases from distributors

questions.explanation: A pull strategy directs promotional effort at consumers through advertising, creating demand that 'pulls' products through the channel — consumers ask retailers for the product.

questions.question: The AIDA model in advertising stands for

options.A: Awareness, Influence, Demand, Action
options.B: Attention, Interest, Desire, Action
options.C: Advertising, Image, Distribution, Appeal
options.D: Attraction, Information, Display, Attitude

questions.explanation: AIDA describes the stages a consumer goes through: Attention (awareness), Interest (interest in the product), Desire (wanting the product), and Action (making the purchase).

questions.question: Above-the-line (ATL) advertising refers to

options.A: Advertising using paid mass media — television, radio, newspapers, and outdoor billboards
options.B: Below-the-line promotional activities such as discounts and coupons
options.C: Advertising directed only at B2B customers
options.D: Advertising above legal limits set by government

questions.explanation: Above-the-line advertising uses paid mass media channels (TV, radio, press, cinema, outdoor). Below-the-line includes sales promotions, direct mail, and POS materials.

questions.question: Which of the following is a disadvantage of television advertising?

options.A: It reaches only a small audience
options.B: It cannot use both sight and sound
options.C: It is very expensive to produce and broadcast
options.D: It is not suitable for consumer goods

questions.explanation: Television advertising is powerful but very expensive to produce and air, making it inaccessible to small businesses.

questions.question: Reach in advertising refers to

options.A: The depth of emotional impact of an advertisement
options.B: The total number of different people exposed to an advertisement at least once
options.C: The number of times an advertisement is repeated to the same audience
options.D: The geographic distance over which an advertisement is broadcast

questions.explanation: Reach is the unduplicated number of different people exposed to an ad at least once during a campaign. Frequency is how many times those people see the ad.

questions.question: Frequency in advertising refers to

options.A: How many different people see an advertisement
options.B: The average number of times a person is exposed to an advertisement during a campaign
options.C: How often a product is advertised in a year
options.D: The number of advertising media used in a campaign

questions.explanation: Frequency measures how many times the average person in the target audience is exposed to an advertisement. Higher frequency builds recall but risks ad fatigue.

questions.question: Institutional advertising aims to

options.A: Promote a specific product or brand
options.B: Promote the image, reputation, or ideas of a company rather than a specific product
options.C: Advertise goods donated to charitable institutions
options.D: Promote products sold exclusively through government institutions

questions.explanation: Institutional (corporate) advertising promotes the company as a whole — its values, reputation, and corporate identity — building goodwill and long-term brand equity.

questions.question: The objective of reminder advertising is to

options.A: Introduce a product to consumers for the first time
options.B: Keep a well-established product in consumers' minds and sustain purchase behaviour
options.C: Persuade consumers to switch from a competing brand
options.D: Build corporate reputation and goodwill

questions.explanation: Reminder advertising is used in the maturity stage to keep consumers aware of the product and maintain brand salience.

questions.question: Consumer behaviour is defined as

options.A: The study of how products are manufactured and distributed
options.B: The study of how individuals and groups select, buy, use, and dispose of goods and services to satisfy needs
options.C: The study of how consumers respond to advertising
options.D: The legal obligations of consumers when purchasing products

questions.explanation: Consumer behaviour examines the buying decision process — what motivates people to buy, how they gather information, evaluate alternatives, purchase, and assess post-purchase satisfaction.

questions.question: Maslow's hierarchy of needs, from the most basic to the highest, is

options.A: Social, safety, physiological, esteem, self-actualisation
options.B: Physiological, safety, social (love/belonging), esteem, self-actualisation
options.C: Safety, physiological, esteem, social, self-actualisation
options.D: Esteem, physiological, social, safety, self-actualisation

questions.explanation: Maslow's hierarchy (1943): (1) Physiological, (2) Safety, (3) Social (love/belonging), (4) Esteem, (5) Self-actualisation.

questions.question: The buying decision process involves which steps in order?

options.A: Purchase, evaluation, need recognition, information search, alternatives evaluation
options.B: Need recognition, information search, evaluation of alternatives, purchase decision, post-purchase behaviour
options.C: Information search, need recognition, purchase, evaluation, alternatives
options.D: Purchase, need recognition, information search, evaluation, post-purchase

questions.explanation: The five-stage buyer decision process: (1) Need recognition, (2) Information search, (3) Evaluation of alternatives, (4) Purchase decision, (5) Post-purchase behaviour.

questions.question: Cognitive dissonance in buying behaviour occurs when

options.A: A consumer is unable to decide between two products
options.B: A buyer experiences discomfort after a purchase, questioning whether they made the right choice
options.C: A consumer becomes addicted to buying unnecessary products
options.D: A consumer changes their mind before completing a purchase

questions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance (post-purchase dissonance) is buyer's remorse — discomfort felt when the product does not fully match expectations or when the consumer second-guesses their decision.

questions.question: Brand loyalty exists when

options.A: A consumer buys a brand once and is satisfied
options.B: A consumer consistently and repeatedly purchases the same brand over time, even when alternatives are available
options.C: A brand has high awareness in the market
options.D: A consumer recommends a brand to friends on one occasion

questions.explanation: Brand loyalty is consistent preference for and repeat purchase of a specific brand. Loyal customers are less price-sensitive and often act as brand advocates.

questions.question: Reference groups influence consumer behaviour by

options.A: Setting government regulations on what can be purchased
options.B: Acting as comparison points that directly or indirectly influence attitudes and buying behaviour
options.C: Providing credit to help consumers purchase expensive items
options.D: Setting the price of goods in the market

questions.explanation: Reference groups (family, friends, celebrities) influence perceptions, aspirations, and brand choices — especially for socially visible products.

questions.question: Habitual buying behaviour occurs when

options.A: A consumer carefully evaluates many alternatives before buying
options.B: A consumer buys a product repeatedly with little involvement or brand comparison
options.C: A consumer makes a high-involvement, infrequent purchase
options.D: A consumer feels significant dissonance after buying

questions.explanation: Habitual buying involves low involvement and little brand differentiation — consumers buy out of habit without much thought.

questions.question: Culture influences buying behaviour because

options.A: Culture sets the legal framework for commerce
options.B: Cultural values, beliefs, and customs shape what people want, how they buy, and what they consider acceptable
options.C: Culture determines the prices consumers are willing to pay
options.D: Cultural factors are controllable by marketers

questions.explanation: Culture is the most fundamental determinant of a person's wants and behaviour. Values and preferences learned from family and society shape consumer choices.

questions.question: Post-purchase evaluation determines

options.A: Whether a consumer will buy the same product again
options.B: How satisfied the consumer is after purchase, influencing repeat buying and word-of-mouth
options.C: The discount the consumer can negotiate on their next purchase
options.D: The government rating of the product's safety

questions.explanation: Post-purchase evaluation compares actual product performance to expectations. Satisfaction drives repurchase; dissatisfaction leads to returns, negative word-of-mouth, and brand switching.

questions.question: Involvement in consumer behaviour refers to

options.A: The number of people involved in a group purchase decision
options.B: The level of personal importance and interest attached to a product or purchase decision
options.C: The consumer's financial investment in a product
options.D: The degree to which a consumer participates in market research

questions.explanation: Involvement is the degree of personal relevance in a product. High involvement (cars, laptops) leads to extensive information search; low involvement (salt, soap) results in habitual buying.

questions.question: The macro environment (PESTEL factors) includes

options.A: The company, suppliers, intermediaries, and competitors
options.B: Political, economic, social, technological, environmental, and legal factors affecting all firms
options.C: Only factors that the firm can directly control
options.D: The firm's internal strengths and weaknesses

questions.explanation: PESTEL examines macro environment external factors affecting all organisations. Firms cannot control these but must adapt to them.

questions.question: The microenvironment in marketing consists of

options.A: Broad societal forces affecting all firms
options.B: Forces close to the company — suppliers, marketing intermediaries, customers, competitors, and publics
options.C: Government policies that regulate the industry
options.D: Technological changes that affect production methods

questions.explanation: The microenvironment includes actors close to the company that affect its ability to serve customers: suppliers, intermediaries, customer markets, competitors, and publics.

questions.question: SWOT analysis in marketing examines

options.A: Political, economic, social, and technological factors only
options.B: Internal strengths and weaknesses, and external opportunities and threats
options.C: Supplier, wholesale, operations, and trade factors
options.D: Sales, wages, overheads, and trade performance

questions.explanation: SWOT evaluates the firm's internal Strengths and Weaknesses and external Opportunities and Threats, informing strategic decisions.

questions.question: A competitor analysis in marketing assesses

options.A: The company's own strengths and weaknesses
options.B: The strategies, strengths, weaknesses, and likely responses of key competitors
options.C: The performance of the company's sales team
options.D: Government policies that restrict competition

questions.explanation: Competitor analysis identifies key rivals, analyses their strategies, and anticipates their actions — helping firms position and build competitive advantages.

questions.question: Technological factors in the marketing environment include

options.A: Interest rates, inflation, and exchange rates
options.B: New inventions, automation, AI, digital platforms, and the pace of technological change
options.C: Government policies and regulations
options.D: Demographic shifts and cultural changes

questions.explanation: Technological factors create opportunities and threats — disrupting industries, enabling new products, changing consumer expectations, and transforming distribution and communication.

questions.question: The BCG (Boston Consulting Group) matrix classifies products based on

options.A: Price and quality
options.B: Market share and market growth rate
options.C: Profit margin and sales volume
options.D: Target market and distribution channel

questions.explanation: The BCG matrix classifies products into Stars (high growth, high share), Cash Cows (low growth, high share), Question Marks (high growth, low share), and Dogs (low growth, low share).

questions.question: A 'cash cow' in the BCG matrix is a product that

options.A: Has high market share and high market growth
options.B: Has high market share but low market growth — generating stable cash flow
options.C: Has low market share and low market growth
options.D: Has low market share but high market growth

questions.explanation: Cash cows are products in mature markets with high share. They generate more cash than they consume, providing funds to invest in stars or question marks.

questions.question: A 'dog' in the BCG matrix is a product that

options.A: Generates high cash but requires little investment
options.B: Has low market share and low market growth — typically generating little profit
options.C: Is new to the market and growing rapidly
options.D: Has high potential but lacks market share

questions.explanation: Dogs have low share in low-growth markets. They usually generate barely enough to maintain themselves. Strategic recommendation is often to harvest or divest.

questions.question: The BCG matrix 'star' product is characterised by

options.A: High market share and low market growth
options.B: High market share and high market growth — requiring heavy investment but generating strong returns
options.C: Low market share and high market growth
options.D: Low market share and low market growth

questions.explanation: Stars have high market share in fast-growing markets. They generate strong returns but need heavy investment to maintain position. They may become cash cows as growth slows.

questions.question: Ansoff's matrix growth strategy involving selling existing products to existing markets is called

options.A: Market development
options.B: Product development
options.C: Market penetration
options.D: Diversification

questions.explanation: Ansoff's matrix: Market penetration = existing products, existing markets (lowest risk). Market development = existing products, new markets. Product development = new products, existing markets. Diversification = new products, new markets (highest risk).

questions.question: Diversification in Ansoff's matrix involves

options.A: Selling more of an existing product in current markets
options.B: Launching new products in new markets — the highest-risk growth strategy
options.C: Introducing a new product to an existing market
options.D: Selling an existing product in new geographic markets

questions.explanation: Diversification is the most risky growth strategy — entering new markets with new products. It requires new capabilities but can provide the greatest growth opportunities.

questions.question: A competitive advantage means

options.A: Being the largest firm in the market
options.B: Having a superior position over competitors that allows a firm to generate greater value for its customers
options.C: Having the lowest production cost in the industry
options.D: Being the first firm to enter a market

questions.explanation: A competitive advantage — through cost leadership, differentiation, or focus — enables a firm to outperform rivals and earn above-average returns.

questions.question: Cost leadership strategy means

options.A: Setting prices lower than all competitors regardless of costs
options.B: Becoming the lowest-cost producer in the industry to compete on price
options.C: Focusing on reducing marketing costs to increase profit
options.D: Outsourcing all production to the cheapest supplier

questions.explanation: Cost leadership means achieving the lowest cost of production — through economies of scale, efficiency, and cost control — enabling lower prices or higher margins.

questions.question: Differentiation strategy means

options.A: Producing many different products for many different markets
options.B: Creating products or services with unique features that customers value and are willing to pay more for
options.C: Selling products at different prices to different customers
options.D: Using different distribution channels to reach different customers

questions.explanation: Differentiation means creating a product perceived as unique — through quality, features, design, service, or brand image — commanding a premium price and building loyalty.

questions.question: Brand extension involves

options.A: Registering a brand in a new country
options.B: Using an existing, successful brand name to launch a new product in a different category
options.C: Extending the warranty period of a brand
options.D: Increasing the advertising budget for an existing brand

questions.explanation: Brand extension uses the equity of an established brand to launch a new product in a different category, reducing launch risk and marketing costs.

questions.question: A private label (own brand) is

options.A: A brand sold exclusively through vending machines
options.B: A brand developed and owned by a retailer or distributor rather than a manufacturer
options.C: A luxury brand available only to private club members
options.D: A brand name registered but not yet launched

questions.explanation: Private labels (store brands) are developed by retailers, usually priced below national brands, helping retailers build loyalty and improve margins.

questions.question: Co-branding involves

options.A: A single company using the same brand name across all its products
options.B: Two or more brands joining together on a single product to leverage each other's equity
options.C: A company launching a second brand to compete with itself
options.D: Using the same brand name in multiple countries

questions.explanation: Co-branding combines two or more well-known brands in a single product — e.g., Nike + Apple for fitness tracking. Each brand lends its equity to the combined offering.

questions.question: Brand positioning involves

options.A: Physically placing branded items at the front of a retail store
options.B: Designing the company's offering and image to occupy a distinct and valued place in the target customer's mind relative to competitors
options.C: Registering a brand name with the intellectual property office
options.D: Allocating a brand to a specific geographic territory

questions.explanation: Brand positioning deliberately creates a specific perception of a brand in consumers' minds. A clear positioning statement differentiates the brand and guides all marketing decisions.

questions.question: Rebranding involves

options.A: Launching a product under a brand name for the first time
options.B: Changing a brand's identity — name, logo, positioning — to refresh its image or appeal to new markets
options.C: Registering a brand trademark in a new country
options.D: Extending a brand into a new product category

questions.explanation: Rebranding changes the brand's identity to better reflect current values, attract new audiences, shed a negative image, or signal a strategic shift.

questions.question: Which characteristic of services means they cannot be stored or inventoried?

options.A: Intangibility
options.B: Inseparability
options.C: Perishability
options.D: Variability

questions.explanation: Perishability means services cannot be stored — an empty airline seat or hotel room is revenue lost forever. This makes supply-demand management critical.

questions.question: Intangibility of services means

options.A: Services are always delivered at low quality
options.B: Services cannot be seen, touched, tasted, or felt before purchase
options.C: Services are produced and consumed simultaneously
options.D: Services vary in quality from one delivery to the next

questions.explanation: Unlike physical products, services are intangible — they cannot be seen or touched before purchase. Marketers address this through tangible cues.

questions.question: Inseparability in services means

options.A: Services are identical each time they are delivered
options.B: Services are produced and consumed simultaneously — provider and customer must be present
options.C: Services cannot be broken down into separate components
options.D: Services cannot be separated from their price

questions.explanation: Inseparability means services are produced and consumed at the same time in the presence of both provider and customer, limiting mass production.

questions.question: Variability (heterogeneity) of services means

options.A: Services are the same every time they are delivered
options.B: The quality of services may vary depending on who provides them, when, where, and how
options.C: Services can be stored and used at a later time
options.D: Service prices vary unpredictably

questions.explanation: Variability means services are inconsistent — quality depends on provider, customer interaction, and context. Service firms standardise processes and train staff to reduce variability.

questions.question: The 'physical evidence' element of the 7Ps of services marketing refers to

options.A: The physical fitness of service employees
options.B: Tangible cues in the service environment — decor, uniforms, brochures, and ambience — that signal quality
options.C: The legal evidence required to operate a service business
options.D: The company's physical assets on the balance sheet

questions.explanation: Physical evidence is the environment in which the service is delivered and tangible representations that help consumers evaluate and experience the service.

questions.question: The 'process' element of the 7Ps of services marketing refers to

options.A: The manufacturing process for producing physical goods
options.B: The procedures, flow of activities, and mechanisms through which a service is delivered to the customer
options.C: The customer complaints handling process
options.D: The process of hiring and training service staff

questions.explanation: Process in services marketing refers to how the service is delivered — the steps, flow, and mechanisms that affect customer experience.

questions.question: Service quality is often measured using the SERVQUAL model, which assesses five dimensions. Which of the following is NOT one of them?

options.A: Reliability
options.B: Responsiveness
options.C: Profitability
options.D: Empathy

questions.explanation: SERVQUAL measures: Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy, and Tangibles. Profitability is not a service quality dimension.

questions.question: Relationship marketing focuses on

options.A: Making a single large sale to the customer
options.B: Building long-term, mutually beneficial relationships with customers and other stakeholders
options.C: Acquiring as many new customers as possible through aggressive promotion
options.D: Setting prices that maintain a stable long-term revenue stream

questions.explanation: Relationship marketing builds long-term customer relationships — increasing lifetime value, loyalty, and advocacy through superior service and personalised communication.

questions.question: Customer Relationship Management (CRM) is

options.A: A government regulatory system for managing consumer complaints
options.B: A strategy and technology for managing all company interactions with current and potential customers to improve relationships and retention
options.C: A method of setting prices based on customer feedback
options.D: A distribution management system for key retail accounts

questions.explanation: CRM uses data and technology to understand and manage customer interactions, tailor communications, and build loyalty — increasing retention and lifetime value.

questions.question: Customer lifetime value (CLV) is

options.A: The total purchases a customer makes in a single transaction
options.B: The total net profit a company expects to earn from a customer over the entire relationship
options.C: The number of years a customer remains loyal to a brand
options.D: The marketing cost of acquiring one new customer

questions.explanation: CLV is the present value of all future profits from a customer relationship. It justifies investment in customer acquisition and retention.

questions.question: Customer acquisition cost (CAC) is

options.A: The cost of producing a product for a new customer
options.B: The total marketing and sales cost divided by the number of new customers acquired
options.C: The price increase needed to cover customer service costs
options.D: The distribution cost of delivering to a new customer

questions.explanation: CAC = Total marketing and sales costs ÷ Number of new customers acquired. It is compared with CLV to assess whether customer acquisition is worthwhile.

questions.question: Industrial (B2B) markets differ from consumer markets mainly because

options.A: They have more buyers and smaller transaction volumes
options.B: Purchases are made by organisations for production, operations, or resale, involving formal buying processes and larger transaction values
options.C: Products are always more expensive than consumer goods
options.D: Marketing is not required in industrial markets

questions.explanation: B2B markets involve fewer but larger buyers, professional purchasing with formal procedures, longer decision cycles, and more complex buyer-seller relationships.

questions.question: Derived demand in industrial markets means

options.A: Demand derived from competitor pricing
options.B: Demand for industrial goods is derived from the demand for the consumer goods they help produce
options.C: Demand artificially stimulated through aggressive marketing
options.D: Demand that does not change with price

questions.explanation: Derived demand means B2B demand depends on consumer demand — e.g., demand for steel is derived from demand for cars. Consumer demand changes ripple back through the supply chain.

questions.question: Globalisation in marketing means

options.A: Selling products only in African markets
options.B: The trend toward a more integrated and interdependent world economy — firms marketing across national boundaries
options.C: Using global pricing to set the same price in all countries
options.D: Manufacturing products in the cheapest country only

questions.explanation: Globalisation is the increasing integration of world markets — firms can source, produce, and sell in multiple countries, opening new markets but also increasing competition.

questions.question: An adapted marketing strategy for international markets involves

options.A: Using exactly the same product and promotion in every country
options.B: Adjusting the product, pricing, promotion, or distribution to suit the specific needs of each target country
options.C: Selling products only in countries with similar cultures
options.D: Using the same distributor in all international markets

questions.explanation: An adapted strategy tailors the marketing mix to each country's cultural, economic, regulatory, and competitive environment.

questions.question: A marketing plan is a document that

options.A: Shows a company's annual financial performance
options.B: Sets out marketing objectives and strategies to achieve them within a specified time frame
options.C: Lists all products offered by the company with their prices
options.D: Records all sales made by the company during the year

questions.explanation: A marketing plan outlines marketing objectives, strategies, and tactics along with budgets and performance measures, typically for one year.

questions.question: A marketing objective should be SMART, meaning

options.A: Strategic, Measurable, Achievable, Resourced, Timed
options.B: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
options.C: Simple, Monitored, Aggressive, Reasonable, Tactical
options.D: Suitable, Manageable, Accurate, Realistic, Tested

questions.explanation: SMART objectives are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound — e.g., 'Increase market share from 10% to 15% within 12 months.'

questions.question: Gap analysis in marketing planning identifies

options.A: Gaps in the company's product portfolio
options.B: The difference between desired future performance and projected current performance — indicating the need for new strategies
options.C: Missing pages in a marketing plan document
options.D: The gap between a firm's price and the market price

questions.explanation: Gap analysis identifies the strategic gap between where the company wants to be and where it is projected to be — highlighting the need for growth strategies.

questions.question: The marketing audit is

options.A: A financial audit of the marketing department's expenditure
options.B: A comprehensive, systematic examination of the marketing environment, objectives, strategies, and activities
options.C: An assessment of each salesperson's performance targets
options.D: A review of advertising campaign expenditure

questions.explanation: A marketing audit is a systematic review of all aspects of marketing — external environment, internal performance, strategy, and organisation — to identify strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats.

questions.question: Ethical marketing requires that firms

options.A: Maximise profits at all costs
options.B: Market products in a way that is honest, fair, and responsible to consumers and society
options.C: Only market products approved by the government
options.D: Avoid all forms of advertising

questions.explanation: Ethical marketing involves honesty in advertising, fair pricing, responsible targeting, and consideration of social and environmental impacts — building long-term trust and brand reputation.

questions.question: False advertising (misleading claims) is unethical because

options.A: It reduces the cost of marketing for companies
options.B: It deceives consumers, causing them to make decisions based on inaccurate information
options.C: It is less effective than truthful advertising
options.D: It is prohibited by competitors' associations

questions.explanation: False advertising misleads consumers, violates their right to accurate information, and can cause financial harm. It is regulated by consumer protection laws.

questions.question: Search Engine Optimisation (SEO) is a digital marketing technique that

options.A: Pays search engines to display advertisements at the top of results
options.B: Improves a website's visibility in organic (unpaid) search engine results by optimising content and structure
options.C: Sends promotional emails to potential customers
options.D: Creates viral content for social media platforms

questions.explanation: SEO optimises website content and structure to rank higher in organic search results. Unlike paid search (PPC), SEO generates unpaid (earned) traffic.

questions.question: Content marketing involves

options.A: Paying for promotional content in newspapers
options.B: Creating and sharing valuable, relevant content (blogs, videos, guides) to attract and engage a target audience
options.C: Producing product packaging that communicates the product's content
options.D: Using celebrities to create content about a product

questions.explanation: Content marketing creates and distributes valuable content to attract a defined audience, building trust and authority — ultimately driving profitable customer action.

questions.question: Social media marketing uses platforms to

options.A: Only broadcast one-way messages to consumers
options.B: Build brand awareness, engage audiences, run targeted ads, and drive traffic or sales through platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok
options.C: Send bulk emails to all customers simultaneously
options.D: Only market to business clients
