questions.explanation: Social media marketing uses various platforms to build brand awareness, engage audiences, run ads, and drive traffic or sales — enabling two-way communication with consumers.

questions.question: Sponsorship as a promotional tool involves

options.A: Paying celebrities to endorse products on social media
options.B: A company financially supporting an event, activity, or person in exchange for brand visibility and positive association
options.C: Offering free samples to consumers at retail outlets
options.D: Providing trade discounts to retailers for prominent display

questions.explanation: Sponsorship provides financial support for events (sports, arts, education) in return for brand exposure and favourable association — building awareness and enhancing brand image.

questions.question: Viral marketing is a strategy that

options.A: Uses computer viruses to send promotional messages
options.B: Encourages consumers to pass on marketing messages to others, creating rapid, exponential spread through networks
options.C: Places advertisements on highly trafficked websites
options.D: Uses biological metaphors in advertising copy

questions.explanation: Viral marketing creates compelling content that consumers voluntarily share, achieving exponential reach at low cost.

questions.question: Word-of-mouth (WOM) is considered highly effective because

options.A: It is the most expensive form of promotion
options.B: It comes from trusted sources (friends, family) and is perceived as more credible than paid advertising
options.C: It can be fully controlled by the marketer
options.D: It reaches the largest number of people simultaneously

questions.explanation: Word-of-mouth is highly credible because it comes from people the recipient knows and trusts, not from a paid source.

questions.question: Influencer marketing involves

options.A: Using well-known personalities to set industry prices
options.B: Partnering with individuals who have a significant and engaged social media following to promote products
options.C: Using government officials to endorse public service products
options.D: Advertising in influential national newspapers only

questions.explanation: Influencer marketing leverages social media personalities who have authority, credibility, and engaged followers in a niche area — their endorsements are seen as more authentic.

questions.question: A loyalty programme (reward scheme) is designed to

options.A: Attract new customers through heavy advertising
options.B: Reward existing customers for repeat purchases, increasing retention and lifetime value
options.C: Give discounts to low-income customers only
options.D: Promote corporate social responsibility activities

questions.explanation: Loyalty programmes reward customers for repeat purchases, increasing switching costs, boosting retention, and gathering valuable purchase data.

questions.question: Consumer sales promotion tools include

options.A: Trade allowances and cooperative advertising
options.B: Coupons, free samples, competitions, loyalty schemes, and buy-one-get-one-free offers
options.C: Sales force incentives and trade shows
options.D: Press releases and editorial features

questions.explanation: Consumer promotions are directed at end buyers to stimulate purchase: coupons, samples, cash-back, competitions, point-of-purchase displays, and loyalty programmes.

questions.question: Trade sales promotion tools are directed at

options.A: Final consumers to encourage immediate purchase
options.B: Retailers and wholesalers to encourage them to stock, display, and promote the manufacturer's products
options.C: The media to generate editorial coverage
options.D: The company's sales representatives

questions.explanation: Trade promotions target intermediaries — offering discounts, free goods, cooperative advertising, and display allowances to encourage distributors and retailers to support the product.

questions.question: Guerrilla marketing involves

options.A: Using military strategy to defeat competitors
options.B: Unconventional, low-cost, high-impact promotional tactics that create surprise, curiosity, or buzz
options.C: Aggressive door-to-door sales campaigns
options.D: Placing advertisements in unexpected locations without permission

questions.explanation: Guerrilla marketing uses surprise, creativity, and unconventional tactics — flash mobs, unusual installations — to create maximum impact on minimal budget.

questions.question: Telemarketing is a form of direct marketing that involves

options.A: Marketing products through television advertisements
options.B: Using the telephone to sell products or services directly to potential customers
options.C: Marketing through mobile apps and SMS only
options.D: Automated marketing through pre-recorded messages only

questions.explanation: Telemarketing uses telephone calls to promote products, generate leads, or take orders directly from consumers.

questions.question: Event marketing involves

options.A: Placing advertisements at sports events only
options.B: Creating, sponsoring, or participating in events to promote a brand and engage directly with the target audience
options.C: Setting up events to train the sales force
options.D: Marketing events management services to corporate clients

questions.explanation: Event marketing promotes a brand by creating brand experiences at events — product launches, festivals, trade shows — generating excitement and direct consumer engagement.

questions.question: Market share is defined as

options.A: The number of products sold by a company in a year
options.B: A company's sales as a percentage of total industry sales
options.C: The share of advertising budget spent relative to competitors
options.D: The proportion of customers who are loyal to a brand

questions.explanation: Market share = (Company's sales ÷ Total industry sales) × 100. It measures a firm's competitive position within its industry.

questions.question: A market leader is a firm that

options.A: Was first to enter the market
options.B: Holds the largest market share and sets the tone for pricing, distribution, and promotion
options.C: Has the most employees in the industry
options.D: Has the highest profit margin in the market

questions.explanation: The market leader holds the largest market share, often sets industry prices, and leads in new products. Examples: Coca-Cola in soft drinks.

questions.question: Porter's Five Forces model analyses

options.A: The four elements of the marketing mix
options.B: The five competitive forces that determine the profitability and attractiveness of an industry
options.C: Five strategies for international market entry
options.D: The five stages of the consumer buying process

questions.explanation: Porter's Five Forces: (1) Threat of new entrants, (2) Threat of substitutes, (3) Bargaining power of buyers, (4) Bargaining power of suppliers, (5) Rivalry among existing competitors.

questions.question: A seller's market is one in which

options.A: There are more buyers than sellers, giving sellers an advantage
options.B: There are more sellers than buyers, giving buyers an advantage
options.C: Supply equals demand in perfect equilibrium
options.D: Government controls prices to protect sellers

questions.explanation: A seller's market has more demand than supply — sellers have market power to charge higher prices. A buyer's market has excess supply, giving buyers negotiating power.

questions.question: Longitudinal research in marketing involves

options.A: Collecting data from a large number of respondents at a single point in time
options.B: Collecting data from the same respondents repeatedly over a long period to track changes
options.C: Conducting research across multiple countries simultaneously
options.D: Studying the long-term effects of advertising

questions.explanation: Longitudinal research tracks the same subjects over time to study trends and changes in behaviour, attitudes, or brand perceptions — used in brand tracking studies and consumer panels.

questions.question: Ethnographic research in marketing involves

options.A: Studying the ethnic composition of a target market
options.B: Researchers immersing themselves in consumers' natural environments to observe their behaviour and lifestyles firsthand
options.C: Using ethnic groups as focus groups for product testing
options.D: Analysing the cultural dimensions of international markets through secondary data

questions.explanation: Ethnographic research involves observing consumers in their natural settings over time to gain deep insights into behaviour, habits, and cultural influences.

questions.question: The research hypothesis in marketing research is

options.A: The final conclusion of the research
options.B: A testable statement about the expected relationship between variables in the research
options.C: A list of research questions to be answered
options.D: The researcher's personal opinion about the topic

questions.explanation: A research hypothesis is a tentative, testable statement predicting a relationship between variables — e.g., 'Increasing advertising spend will increase brand awareness.'

questions.question: Cluster sampling is a probability sampling technique where

options.A: Every nth person on a list is chosen
options.B: The population is divided into clusters, a random sample of clusters is selected, then members of selected clusters are included
options.C: The researcher fills predetermined quotas from specified subgroups
options.D: Every member of the population has an equal chance of selection

questions.explanation: Cluster sampling divides the population into clusters and randomly selects clusters. It is more economical for geographically dispersed populations.

questions.question: Product standardisation means

options.A: Setting government quality standards for products
options.B: Offering the same product with no modifications to all markets or customer segments
options.C: Producing goods to a minimum quality standard only
options.D: Testing products against industry benchmarks

questions.explanation: Product standardisation delivers the same product globally without adaptation, achieving economies of scale and consistent branding.

questions.question: The concept of product obsolescence refers to

options.A: A product becoming unsafe for consumer use
options.B: A product becoming out of date, unfashionable, or replaced by superior alternatives
options.C: The process of recycling old products
options.D: A legal restriction on selling outdated products

questions.explanation: Product obsolescence occurs when a product is no longer desirable because of changing tastes, newer technology, or better alternatives. Planned obsolescence is a deliberate marketing strategy.

questions.question: A product recall occurs when

options.A: A product is returned to the warehouse after the sales season ends
options.B: A company retrieves a product from the market due to a safety defect or quality problem
options.C: A retailer returns unsold goods to the manufacturer
options.D: A consumer returns a product for a refund

questions.explanation: A product recall is a request by a manufacturer to return a product due to safety concerns or defects. It protects consumers but damages brand reputation if not managed well.

questions.question: Product labelling serves which functions?

options.A: Only to comply with legal requirements
options.B: To identify the product, describe its contents, provide usage instructions, and promote the brand
options.C: To increase the weight and size of the product
options.D: To make the product more expensive for consumers

questions.explanation: Labels identify the product and brand, describe contents, ingredients and expiry dates, provide instructions, promote the brand, and comply with legal requirements.

questions.question: Geographic pricing adjusts prices based on

options.A: The time of day the product is purchased
options.B: The buyer's location — covering delivery costs or reflecting different market conditions in different regions
options.C: The age and income of the buyer
options.D: The volume of the order placed by the buyer

questions.explanation: Geographic pricing accounts for differences in location to ensure fair pricing relative to delivery costs and local market conditions.

questions.question: Tender pricing is used when

options.A: A firm wants to set the lowest possible price in the market
options.B: A firm submits a sealed bid price to win a specific contract, often in public procurement
options.C: A firm sets prices based on customer demand patterns
options.D: Prices are set by government regulatory authorities

questions.explanation: Tender (sealed bid) pricing is used when companies submit competitive bids for contracts, balancing a low enough price to win with a high enough price to make profit.

questions.question: Price leadership occurs when

options.A: The cheapest firm sets the price for all competitors
options.B: A dominant firm sets the price that other firms in the industry tend to follow
options.C: The government sets prices for all firms in an industry
options.D: All firms simultaneously raise their prices

questions.explanation: Price leadership is when the market leader sets the price and competitors follow, avoiding price wars in oligopolistic markets.

questions.question: Demographic factors that affect marketing decisions include

options.A: Interest rates and inflation
options.B: Population size, age structure, urbanisation, and household composition
options.C: Government regulations and trade policies
options.D: Technological innovation and digital transformation

questions.explanation: Demographic factors (population size, age distribution, urban/rural split, birth rates, household size) directly affect demand for products and help firms target the right segments.

questions.question: The family life cycle influences buying behaviour because

options.A: Families have more buying power than individuals
options.B: Consumers' needs, priorities, and spending patterns change as they move through different life stages
options.C: Families always buy more expensive products
options.D: Family members always agree on what to buy

questions.explanation: The family life cycle (young single, young married, parents, empty nesters, retired) influences buying behaviour — priorities and spending patterns change at each stage.

questions.question: Opinion leaders influence consumer behaviour because they

options.A: Set the prices of goods in the market
options.B: Are trusted sources whose views and recommendations are respected by their social groups
options.C: Are government officials who approve advertising
options.D: Manufacture the products that consumers buy

questions.explanation: Opinion leaders are influential members of reference groups whose views are respected. Word-of-mouth from opinion leaders is one of the most powerful forms of promotion.

questions.question: Market development in Ansoff's matrix involves

options.A: Selling more of an existing product in existing markets
options.B: Selling existing products to new markets or new segments
options.C: Introducing new products to existing markets
options.D: Launching new products in new markets

questions.explanation: Market development = existing products to new markets (new geographic regions, new customer segments). It is the second-lowest risk Ansoff strategy after market penetration.

questions.question: Product development in Ansoff's matrix involves

options.A: Selling existing products to new markets
options.B: Developing new products for existing markets
options.C: Entering new markets with new products
options.D: Increasing sales of existing products in existing markets

questions.explanation: Product development = new or modified products offered to existing markets. It leverages existing customer relationships while introducing new products.

questions.question: A wholesaler adds value in the distribution channel by

options.A: Setting the final price consumers pay for products
options.B: Performing sorting, storing, transporting, financing, risk-bearing, and information-sharing functions
options.C: Manufacturing goods on behalf of retailers
options.D: Advertising directly to consumers to drive traffic to retailers

questions.explanation: Wholesalers add value through bulk-breaking, storage, credit financing for retailers, risk-bearing, transportation, and market information, enabling manufacturers to reach widely dispersed retailers efficiently.

questions.question: The advertising agency's creative brief is a document that

options.A: Summarises the agency's billing to the client
options.B: Outlines the objectives, target audience, key message, tone, and deliverables for an advertising campaign
options.C: Describes the media plan for distributing an advertisement
options.D: Provides a legal agreement between the agency and the client

questions.explanation: A creative brief outlines campaign objectives, target audience, key insight, message, tone, and executional requirements — guiding the creative team in developing the advertisement.

questions.question: Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI) measures

options.A: The total profit generated by the company in a financial year
options.B: The financial return generated by marketing activities relative to the cost of those activities
options.C: The return on investment from advertising only
options.D: The total market value of a company's brand portfolio

questions.explanation: ROMI = (Revenue attributed to marketing – Marketing costs) ÷ Marketing costs × 100. It assesses the efficiency of marketing spending in generating revenue.

questions.question: A brand mark (logo) is

options.A: The spoken part of the brand — the name
options.B: The visual symbol or design that identifies the brand and cannot be spoken
options.C: The registered trademark number of the brand
options.D: The slogan used in advertising the brand

questions.explanation: A brand mark is the visual element (logo, symbol, icon) that identifies the brand but cannot be spoken — e.g., Nike's swoosh or Apple's bitten apple.

questions.question: A want in marketing is defined as

options.A: A basic state of felt deprivation
options.B: A human need shaped by culture, personality, and individual experience
options.C: A demand backed by purchasing power
options.D: A government regulation on what consumers must buy

questions.explanation: A want is a need shaped by culture and individual personality — e.g., a need for food becomes a want for a specific cuisine. Wants become demands when backed by purchasing power.

questions.question: Positioning statement defines

options.A: Where a product will be sold geographically
options.B: The target segment and the key benefit or difference a brand offers relative to competitors
options.C: The price at which a product will be positioned in the market
options.D: The advertising budget allocated to promote a product

questions.explanation: A positioning statement communicates the target market, frame of reference, point of difference, and why consumers should believe the brand's claim.

questions.question: Product depth refers to

options.A: The number of different product lines in a company's mix
options.B: The number of versions or variants offered within each product line
options.C: The total number of individual products in all product lines
options.D: The quality level of a product compared to competitors

questions.explanation: Product depth refers to the number of variants in a product line — e.g., a shampoo line offered in 5 sizes and 3 formulations has a depth of 15 items.

questions.question: Optional product pricing involves

options.A: Selling a main product at a low price and making profit on optional extras
options.B: Offering the same product at different prices in different markets
options.C: Setting no fixed price — allowing customers to decide what to pay
options.D: Pricing products as part of a bundle

questions.explanation: Optional product pricing charges a base price for a product and extra for optional accessories or features — the main product generates traffic; the extras generate profit.

questions.question: Which of the following marketing strategies can help manage the perishability of services?

options.A: Using more attractive product packaging
options.B: Differential pricing, advance reservations, and demand stimulation during off-peak periods
options.C: Increasing product inventory during peak demand
options.D: Reducing the number of service outlets

questions.explanation: To manage perishability, service marketers use differential pricing (e.g., off-peak discounts), advance reservations, and demand stimulation during slow periods — matching supply and demand.

questions.question: Digital (online) advertising advantages over traditional media include

options.A: Higher production costs and longer lead times
options.B: Precise targeting, real-time performance tracking, interactivity, and lower minimum budgets
options.C: Guaranteed high viewership regardless of content quality
options.D: Inability to measure return on investment

questions.explanation: Digital advertising enables precise audience targeting, real-time tracking of clicks and conversions, interactive formats, and lower entry costs compared to TV or print.

questions.question: The marketing budget is

options.A: The total profit target of the marketing department
options.B: The financial plan allocating resources to different marketing activities over a specified period
options.C: The government's allocation to national marketing campaigns
options.D: The revenue generated by the marketing department in a year

questions.explanation: The marketing budget specifies how funds are allocated across marketing activities — advertising, research, sales force, promotions, PR — aligning spending with strategic priorities.

questions.question: Franchising as a distribution method means

options.A: Selling goods on credit to retailers
options.B: The franchisor grants franchisees the right to use its brand and business system in exchange for fees
options.C: A manufacturer selling directly to consumers
options.D: Distributing goods through government-approved agents only

questions.explanation: In a franchise distribution system, the franchisor grants the franchisee the right to sell its products using its brand, systems, and support — in return for fees and royalties.

questions.question: The psychological factor of perception influences buying because

options.A: It determines how much money consumers are willing to spend
options.B: Consumers select, organise, and interpret information in ways that create a personal picture of the world — affecting what they buy and how they respond to marketing
options.C: Perception is the same for all consumers in a market
options.D: It only affects consumer choices for luxury goods

questions.explanation: Perception is the process by which people select and interpret stimuli — two consumers can have different perceptions of the same product, affecting their buying decisions differently.

questions.question: The term 'first mover advantage' in marketing means

options.A: The benefit of being the first company to reduce prices in a market
options.B: The competitive advantage gained by being the first to enter a new market or introduce a new product
options.C: The advantage of being the first company to advertise on a new media platform
options.D: The benefit of being the first retailer to open in a new geographic area

questions.explanation: First mover advantage is the competitive edge gained by entering a market early — establishing brand recognition, customer loyalty, and resource control before competitors arrive.

questions.question: The marketing mix is described as 'controllable' variables because

options.A: They are set by government regulations
options.B: They are internal factors that the firm can adjust and control to achieve its marketing objectives
options.C: They control the behaviour of consumers in the market
options.D: They are fixed once a marketing plan is developed

questions.explanation: The marketing mix (4Ps/7Ps) consists of variables within the firm's control — unlike external PESTEL and competitive forces. Firms adjust the mix to respond to market conditions and customer needs.
